questions.question: Define Entity, Entity Set, and Entity Type in DBMS with examples.
keyPoints: Entity: Real-world object that can be uniquely identified (e.g., Student, Employee)
Entity Type: Abstract definition/schema of a group of entities (e.g., STUDENT)
Entity Set: Collection of all entities of a particular type at a given time

questions.question: Explain the different types of attributes in ER modeling with examples.
keyPoints: Simple: Cannot be divided further (e.g., Age, Salary)
Composite: Can be divided (e.g., Address → Street, City)
Single-valued: One value per entity (e.g., DateOfBirth)
Multi-valued: Multiple values (e.g., PhoneNumber)
Derived: Computed from others (e.g., Age from DOB)

questions.question: What is a Weak Entity? How is it different from a Strong Entity? Give an example.
keyPoints: Weak Entity: Depends on strong entity for identification, no primary key of its own
Strong Entity: Exists independently, has its own primary key
Example: Dependent (weak) depends on Employee (strong)
Weak entity uses owner's PK + partial key as identifier
Represented by double rectangle in ER diagram

questions.question: Differentiate between Total Participation and Partial Participation with examples.
keyPoints: Total Participation: Every entity MUST participate in the relationship (mandatory)
Partial Participation: Entity MAY or MAY NOT participate (optional)
Example Total: Every student must enroll in a course
Example Partial: An employee may or may not manage a department
Notation: Double line = total, single line = partial

questions.question: Explain the three types of Cardinality ratios in ER modeling.
keyPoints: 1:1 (One-to-One): One entity relates to exactly one entity
1:N (One-to-Many): One entity relates to many entities
M:N (Many-to-Many): Many entities relate to many entities
Example 1:1: One passport belongs to one person
Example 1:N: One department has many employees
Example M:N: Students enroll in many courses

questions.question: Define Primary Key, Candidate Key, and Foreign Key with examples.
keyPoints: Primary Key: Unique identifier, cannot be NULL (e.g., StudentID)
Candidate Key: Potential PK, all unique attributes (e.g., RollNo, Email)
Foreign Key: References PK of another table, creates relationship
Super Key: Set of attributes that uniquely identify (may have extras)
Example FK: DeptID in Employee referencing Department table

questions.question: Draw an ER Diagram for a Hospital Management System. The system has Patients, Doctors, and Wards. A patient is admitted to a ward and is treated by a doctor. A doctor can treat multiple patients. A ward has multiple patients but at any time a patient is in one ward. Include at least 3 attributes for each entity and specify keys and cardinalities.
keyPoints: Entities: PATIENT (PatientID-PK, Name, Age, Disease), DOCTOR (DoctorID-PK, Name, Specialization), WARD (WardNo-PK, Capacity, Type)
Relationships: Admitted_In (Patient-Ward), Treats (Doctor-Patient)
Cardinalities: Doctor-Patient = 1:N, Patient-Ward = 1:N, Ward-Patient = 1:N
Note: Treats relationship is binary 1:N or M:N if patient sees multiple doctors
Total Participation: Patient must be admitted to some ward
Weak Entities: None in this simplified version

questions.question: Explain the concept of N-ary relationships in ER modeling. Differentiate between Binary, Ternary, and Quaternary relationships with suitable examples.
keyPoints: N-ary: Relationship connecting N entity sets
Binary (N=2): Two entity sets - e.g., Student enrols in Course
Ternary (N=3): Three entity sets - e.g., Supplier provides Parts to Project
Quaternary (N=4): Four entity sets - complex relationships
Ternary example: Employee works on Project using Skill
Transformation: M:N relationships become separate junction tables
Important: Ternary ≠ three binary relationships - semantics differ
Crow's foot notation uses diamond with N lines

questions.question: Consider a Library Management System with Entities: BOOK, AUTHOR, PUBLISHER, and MEMBER. Write down the business rules and convert the ER Diagram to relational tables, specifying Primary Keys and Foreign Keys.
keyPoints: Entities: BOOK(BookID-PK, Title, ISBN, PubYear), AUTHOR(AuthorID-PK, Name), PUBLISHER(PublisherID-PK, Name), MEMBER(MemberID-PK, Name)
Relationships: Written_By (Book-Author, M:N), Published_By (Book-Publisher, 1:N), Borrowed (Book-Member, M:N)
Junction tables needed for M:N: Written_By(BookID, AuthorID), Borrowed(BookID, MemberID, BorrowDate)
Foreign Keys: Book.PublisherID → Publisher.PublisherID, Junction table FKs reference both entities
Key decisions: Derived attributes not stored, Multi-valued become separate tables

questions.question: What is an ER Diagram? Explain the Chen notation symbols used in ER modeling with their meanings and an example for each.
keyPoints: ER Diagram: Visual representation of database schema showing entities, attributes, relationships
Rectangle: Entity set (e.g., STUDENT)
Ellipse: Attribute (e.g., Name, Age)
Double Ellipse: Multi-valued (e.g., PhoneNumber)
Dashed Ellipse: Derived (e.g., Age)
Diamond: Relationship set (e.g., Enrolled_In)
Double Rectangle: Weak entity (e.g., Dependent)
Line: Links entities to attributes and relationships
Example: Rectangle(STUDENT) → Ellipse(Name) connected via line to Diamond(Enrolled_In) → Rectangle(COURSE)
